votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, checking if any of the three integers is the sum of the other two when all inputs are NumInt, and returning false otherwise.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, checking if any of the three integers is the sum of the other two when all inputs are NumInt, and returning false otherwise.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, checking if the three inputs are NumInts and whether one is the sum of the other two, returning the expected boolean equality.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. It checks if all three inputs are NumInt instances, and if so, computes whether res equals the boolean expression checking if any one of the three integers equals the sum of the other two. If any input is not NumInt, it checks that res is False. This matches the Coq specification exactly for all concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. It checks if all three inputs are NumInt instances, and if so, computes whether res equals the boolean expression checking if any one number equals the sum of the other two. If any input is not NumInt, it checks that res is False. This matches the Coq specification exactly for all concrete inputs.

Both compute whether res equals the boolean that any one of the three equalities xi=yi+zi, yi=xi+zi, or zi=yi+xi holds when all inputs are integers, and require res=false otherwise. Python ints match Coq Z semantics.

Matches Coq: when all are NumInt, checks if one equals sum of other two; otherwise requires res = false. Python uses arbitrary-precision ints and returns whether res equals that boolean, aligning with the Coq predicate.
Matches Coq: when all are NumInt, checks res equals (xi==yi+zi or yi==xi+zi or zi==yi+xi); otherwise requires res=false. Python ints mirror Coq Z.
